name: Christian Butler
title: What We Miss Most
story: When Perla lived with us, the daily rhythms were morning prayers and devotions together, meals together, TV and conversation, her curiosity, her questions, taking care of her. Three unexpected gifts came from living together: the small sacred moments of caring for her, watching her faith up close, and she brought the family closer because she needed help, which required everyone to pull together. What I miss most: her voice, her questions, her curiosity, praying together. Her sounds, taking care of her. We are going to miss that.

name: Ciria Arellano-Butler
title: The Songs She Sang
story: Ciria's favorite memories are the songs Perla would sing. She taught childhood nursery rhymes, TV jingles, and church hymns to the kids. She later introduced those same songs to her granddaughters Chimena and Angelina. Perla's nieces Crissy and Jennifer had extended sleepover weekends with Perla. PJ, Perla's God Son, also received Auntie Perla's unconditional love. Perla's greatest passion was her family, especially her children, all the kids in the family.

name: The Butler Family
title: Going Back to Grandma's
story: Leaving Grandma and Pompo's house one night, driving on 120th between Sheridan and Lowell, little Chimena threw a temper tantrum in her car seat. She tried to open the door, take off her seat belt, and get out of the car in the middle of traffic to go back to Grandma's house. That's how much she wanted to stay.

name: Christian Butler
title: The Via Dolorosa
story: Perla taught me St. Francis of Assisi, St. Teresa of Avila, St. John of the Cross, the great Mystics. She is responsible for my meditation practice, my contemplative prayer. She helped build the meditation walk at the Shrine and helped that energy thrive. She would pray the Rosary with me. She would pray the Via Della Rosa at Easter. We walked the Stations of the Cross together at the Shrine, walking up the stairs, and here at Spirit of Christ, walking around the church. When I walked the actual Via Dolorosa in Jerusalem, the road where Christ carried His cross, I walked it thinking of her. Everything she taught me about the Mystics, everything she taught me about Christ, I saw firsthand in the Holy Land.

name: The Butler Family
title: Canned Goods in the Trunk
story: Perla gave things away constantly. Clothes, household items, whatever someone needed. She kept canned goods in the trunk of her car to give to people asking for money on the side of the road. She was also a great fundraiser for the Shrine and for the church. She had no problem asking people who had means to contribute. She wouldn't guilt them. She would influence them with her love and her energy.

name: The Butler Family
title: Their First Date
story: Jerry Arellano was a manager at a popular Denver dance club. That is where Perla met him. He was handsome, charismatic. Their first date was at the Urban Farmer. When they were leaving, Jerry went to the restroom. Perla was waiting outside. It was winter, snowing, cold. She was wearing a rabbit fur coat she had saved up for, something very valuable to her, and a red 70s strap dress. A woman nearby was cold. Perla gave her the coat. Jerry came out of the restroom and his date was standing in the snow in a red dress with no coat. That was their first date. That was Perla.

name: Christian Butler
title: The Woman Who Showed Us the Way
story:
I met Perla when I was fifteen years old. I was a sophomore. Ciria was a junior. I was in love with Ciria's talent, her beauty, and her faith. She was a believer who wasn't shy about Jesus. I was not a believer. I thought people who believed in God were weak-minded.

Perla looked at me, this kid who had opinions about her faith, and told Ciria I was a nice boy. Right in front of me. At fifteen, that disarmed me. It made me feel seen. And it made me want to be worthy of it.

I have spent the rest of my life trying to be worthy of what Perla Arellano saw in me.

She was relentless, but never harsh. She led with love, but she wasn't going to leave your soul up to chance. That was her gift. Not just to me, but to everyone. She met you where you were. She loved you before you earned it. And through that love, she opened doors that arguments never could.

"It's my job to help you get to heaven."

That's what one of the Missionary Sisters told the girls at Queen of Heaven Orphanage. Perla was born on Halloween, 1952, in Greeley, Colorado. She was raised in Denver by the Missionary Sisters of the Sacred Heart of Jesus. More than two hundred orphan girls, a handful of nuns, and a whole lot of love. Mary Pelikan was right beside Perla from the cribs. They had no memories of life without each other. Mary wasn't a friend. She was her sister.

Every summer, the girls would go up to the Stone House on Lookout Mountain. The mountains, the outdoors, the freedom. That was where Perla was happiest as a child.

Then the orphanage closed. She was fourteen. The world she knew was gone.

She moved through foster homes. She worked a donut factory in high school. She waitressed to pay rent. She walked a picket line at Continental Airlines with her kids in roller skates at the union hall. Every job was in service to something larger than herself.

And then, the full circle. In 1995, Perla went back to the Shrine on Lookout Mountain and worked there for twenty-three years. A girl from the orphanage who came home.

Jerry Arellano was a manager at a popular Denver dance club. That is where Perla met him. He was handsome, charismatic. They were married in 1974 and they were a team for over fifty years. They raised Ciria and Adam. She became Grandma to Chimena and Angelina. That was her greatest joy.

Ciria sang at her mother's funeral. That tells you everything about the woman Perla raised.

Adam preceded her in death. At his burial, she threw a popcorn ball into the grave. Because he liked them.

She had so much darkness in her life. And she chose to be the light.

"You are the light of the world. A city set on a hill cannot be hidden." — Matthew 5:14

She was not someone who had an easy life and therefore found it easy to love. She had every reason to be bitter, to be guarded. And she was none of those things. She was wide open. She was radiant. She was a human made of pure love.

She is responsible for my faith. She taught me St. Francis of Assisi, St. Teresa of Avila, St. John of the Cross. She taught me contemplative prayer. She walked the Stations of the Cross with me at Spirit of Christ and up the stairs at the Shrine.

When I walked the Via Dolorosa in Jerusalem, the actual road where Christ carried His cross, I walked it thinking of her. Everything she taught me, I saw firsthand in the Holy Land. Perla was with me on every step.

She felt like my mother. She invested in me. She built me up publicly and corrected me privately, because she loved me. I am who I am because of her.

She was also the funniest person in any room. She danced like Beyonce during Super Bowl 50. Sixty-three years old, dancing in her living room while the Broncos won the Super Bowl. She jumped in leaf piles at seventy-one. She threw dinner rolls in dark movie theaters. She kept canned goods in the trunk of her car to give to anyone who needed them.

The weight and the lightness, held together in the same breath. The childlike spirit that Jesus told us to have.

"Truly I tell you, unless you change and become like little children, you will never enter the kingdom of heaven." — Matthew 18:3

Perla's most cherished roles, in her own words: Wife. Mom. Auntie. Friend. And her greatest joy, Grandma.

"Well done, good and faithful servant." — Matthew 25:21

She is singing now. She is home. And for those of us still here, she showed us the way.

If Perla touched your life, we would love to hear your story. Use the Share a Memory button on this page to tell us what she meant to you. Every memory matters. Every story keeps her with us.

name: Christian Butler
title: Walking Into Her Home at Fifteen
story: I met Perla when I was fifteen years old. The house was warm, a little loud, and had Christ's presence. But it wasn't overt. There were crosses on the wall; you could see it if you had a discerning eye. It was an open and welcoming house with Christ's presence and love there if you could see it. She looked at me, this kid who had opinions about her faith, and told Ciria I was a nice boy. Right in front of me. At fifteen, that disarmed me. It made me feel seen. And it made me want to be worthy of it. I have spent the rest of my life trying to be worthy of what Perla Arellano saw in me.

name: The Butler Family
title: Canned Goods in the Trunk
story: Perla gave things away constantly. Clothes, household items, whatever someone needed. She kept canned goods in the trunk of her car to give to people asking for money on the side of the road. She was also a great fundraiser for the Shrine and for the church. She had no problem asking people who had means to contribute. She wouldn't guilt them. She would influence them with her love and her energy.

name: The Butler Family
title: Their First Date
story: Jerry Arellano was a manager at a popular Denver dance club. That is where Perla met him. He was handsome, charismatic. Their first date was at the Urban Farmer. When they were leaving, Jerry went to the restroom. Perla was waiting outside. It was winter, snowing, cold. She was wearing a rabbit fur coat she had saved up for, something very valuable to her, and a red 70s strap dress. A woman nearby was cold. Perla gave her the coat. Jerry came out of the restroom and his date was standing in the snow in a red dress with no coat. That was their first date. That was Perla.

name: The Butler Family
title: The Sacred Ordinary
story: A quiet afternoon watching golf together. She kept asking if the players had to pay to play and if the winner made money. Not really about the money, just trying to stay connected. The kind of moment that seems ordinary until you realize you'll never get another one.

name: The Butler Family
title: Conversation with Perla
story: A wide-ranging conversation while her hair was being brushed. The sisters at the orphanage. Being called "the baby" by the family. Stories from a life fully lived, preserved because someone thought to press record.

name: The Butler Family
title: Father's Day Voicemail
story: Perla left a voicemail wishing Christian a happy Father's Day, telling him how much she loved him. Her voice, still here.

name: The Butler Family
title: The Morning Call
story: Perla or Jerry always called Ciria first thing in the morning. Every single day. It was who they were. That connected, sending love in ways they weren't even fully aware of.

name: Briana Janelle Butler
title: Briana Janelle's Facebook Post
story: In Perla's last week, she looked Briana in the eyes. Told her she was special. To do great things. Be good to all people. Fight for women's advocacy. They cried together. Then Perla started making jokes. That was her. The weight and the lightness, held together in the same breath.

name: The Butler Family
title: The Servant Song
story: A song Perla and Christian shared. It was Christian's favorite song at church. Ciria, Chimena, and Angelina recorded it together when the girls were little as a Father's Day gift for Christian. Perla loved the recording and she loved the lyrics. The same recording was played at Adam's funeral Mass at Mount Olivet.

name: The Butler Family
title: The Cabrini Garden Statue
story: In the Cabrini Garden at Mother Cabrini Shrine, there is a statue of Mother Cabrini gently guiding two young orphans. One of the orphan figures was modeled after Ciria, because there were no childhood photos of Perla. She was an orphan; they didn't take many pictures. The artist used photos of Ciria as the reference for the child. Perla's daughter, standing in for her at the place that defined her life.

name: The Butler Family
title: The Stone House
story: The Stone House at Mother Cabrini Shrine is where Perla and Mary spent their happiest childhood summers. The freedom of the outdoors, the mountains, the animals, each other. When the orphanage closed in 1967, the summer camp ended and that part of her life was gone. But decades later, because Perla worked at the Shrine, she could get the Stone House for the family. Christian and Ciria would go up with their own friends and stay there over weekends. The same dormitory where Perla had slept as an orphan girl became the place where her daughter and son-in-law made their own memories.

name: The Butler Family
title: The Mother Cabrini Shrine Gala
story: Christian and Ciria attended the annual Mother Cabrini Shrine Gala with Perla in the late 1990s and 2000s, the good years, when Perla was mobile. Adele Arakawa from 9News MC'd the event. Reggie Rivers, former Denver Bronco, MC'd one year. Kyle Dyer now produces video stories for the gala through Kyle Dyer Storytelling. Perla wasn't just attending. Her work at the Shrine in community outreach, special events, and donation procurement was the engine behind these events.

name: The Butler Family
title: Father John Lager & The Brown Robe Benefit
story: Father John Lager, OFM Cap., is delivering Perla's homily on Monday. He is a Capuchin Franciscan friar. The 33rd Annual Brown Robe Benefit is scheduled for Saturday, October 17, 2026, which is Jerry and Perla's wedding anniversary date. The save-the-date card was mailed to Perla Arellano and arrived after she passed.

name: The Butler Family
title: Jerry & Perla, October 17, 1974
story: The urn is a layered wood carving depicting Jerry and Perla sitting together, looking out at mountains with a covered bridge in the background. Their wedding date, October 17, 1974, is engraved on it.

name: The Butler Family
title: How She Changed People
story: Perla had a way of meeting people exactly where they were. She was relentless, but never harsh. She led with love, but she wasn't going to leave your soul up to chance. She was both extremely patient and forceful at the same time, depending on the moment. She drove hard toward love. Through that love, she opened doors to faith that arguments never could. Her son-in-law's Catholic confirmation name is Francis, because of St. Francis of Assisi, because of her.

name: The Butler Family
title: Original Pizza & Los Arcos
story: Two family restaurants woven into the fabric of life with Perla. Original Pizza, run by Carmela and Ralph, an Italian family who started in 1991. Los Arcos Mexican Restaurant, run by Kendra and John, family-owned since 1978 on W 80th Ave in Westminster. These weren't just places to eat. They were places where love showed up, where community was built, where Perla made everyone feel like family.

name: Father David
title: Father David Blue Jacket
story: Father David shared the story of attending the 2008 Democratic National Convention with Perla. When told only one companion could sit with her in the handicapped section, Perla hid her badge, performed an Oscar-worthy act of dismay, got a replacement, and gave Father David the original, getting everyone great seats near the stage. "And the Academy Award for Best Performance by an Actress goes to Perla." She just smiled.

name: Joseph R. Gomez
title: Joseph R. Gomez
story: Joseph Gomez knew Perla through the men's retreat at Mother Cabrini Shrine in the Stone House. She moved the men deeply with her testimony, her own story, told in the very building where she had slept as an orphan girl. He wrote: she was "a beautiful woman with a huge heart and gentle soul," "truly indwelled by the Holy Spirit," and "the hands and face of Christ to whomever she met."

name: The Butler Family
title: "I'm Not Bossy, I Just Know What You Should Be Doing"
story: Perla got things done. She would take on tasks and big projects with a confidence that they would get done, especially if it benefited a purpose she believed in. She was once gifted a shirt that read: "I'm not bossy, I just know what you should be doing." That was Perla.

name: The Butler Family
title: Jumping in Leaf Piles
story: Perla jumped in leaf piles a few years ago. There's great video and pictures. She really wasn't in the condition to do it, but she did it anyway. She was 71 years old but full of youthful joy.

name: The Butler Family
title: Dancing Like Beyonce, Super Bowl 50
story: During the halftime show of Super Bowl 50, Denver Broncos vs. Carolina Panthers, Broncos won 24-10, Perla danced like Beyonce. There is video, and it is absolutely hysterical. She was 63 years old, dancing in her living room while the Broncos won the Super Bowl. Pure Perla.

name: The Butler Family
title: The Claw
story: During sleepovers, Grandma Perla would pretend her arm was "the Claw," picking up toys and dropping them on the bed. The kind of silly, physical, fully-present grandma game that kids never forget.

name: The Butler Family
title: Dinner Rolls, Popcorn Balls, and a Life Fully Lived
story: She would throw dinner rolls in a dark movie theater. At Adam's burial, she threw a popcorn ball into the hole with his casket because he liked them. She did what she wanted. She was so full of life. She always made people laugh, and she always got away with so much because of her beautiful, wonderful, vibrant, childlike nature. The childlike nature that Jesus Christ told us to be like.
